comments:
This paper proposes PTransE, an extension of TransE that incorporates multi-step relation paths into knowledge-base embedding learning. Two main technical contributions are: (1) a path-constraint resource allocation (PCRA) algorithm to score the reliability of relation paths between an entity pair; and (2) methods to represent relation paths via composition of relation embeddings (addition, multiplication, and RNN). The composed path vectors are integrated into a margin-based TransE-style objective; the model also incorporates Pr(r|p) from training data when scoring. The authors evaluate on Freebase-derived datasets (FB15K/FB40K) for entity and relation prediction and on NYT for relation extraction, showing consistent improvements over TransE and several baselines. The paper is well structured and the motivation is clear: many useful inference patterns in KBs arise from multi-step paths and should be captured by embeddings.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important limitation of TransE by modeling multi-step paths; the idea to treat paths as translations is natural and effective. 
- PCRA is a reasonable heuristic to down-weight noisy/less-informative paths, and the use of Pr(r|p) further refines path utility.
- Empirical evaluation is comprehensive within the presented scope: entity prediction, relation prediction, and distant supervision relation extraction. Results show meaningful gains, and comparisons include recent baselines (TransH, TransR, etc.).
- The paper reports implementation details (reverse relations, path length limits, thresholds) and provides a code link, aiding reproducibility.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Path selection relies on several heuristics (length capped at 3, reliability threshold 0.01, re-ranking top-500 candidates for entity prediction). These are sensible for efficiency but their effect on performance and coverage should be analyzed more thoroughly. An ablation that isolates the contribution of PCRA (vs. unweighted path inclusion) would strengthen the empirical claims.
- The RNN composition option is described but seems less effective and more costly; more analysis on when and why RNN helps or hurts would be useful. Complexity analysis notes O(SKPL) vs O(SK^2PL) for RNN, but measured runtime/memory or scaling experiments are missing.
- Some experimental choices are pragmatic (re-ranking using TransE scores) and could bias results; fuller experiments without re-ranking would clarify general applicability, albeit with higher cost.
- While comparisons to many baselines are present, an explicit ablation showing the impact of each component (PCRA, composition choice, Pr(r|p)) is lacking.

Overall, this is a solid and well-motivated extension of TransE that meaningfully leverages path information. The methodological choices are sensible, experiments indicate clear gains, and the work is a useful contribution to representation learning for KBs. I recommend acceptance with minor requests to add more ablation/analysis of the path-selection heuristics, computational costs, and component-wise contributions.
